learning_outcomes.description: Apply economic theories and frameworks to critical environmental problems within the interdisciplinary industry.
Implement market and non-market tools and techniques to improve environmental quality and reduce environmental destruction.
Distinguish various environmental problems locally and globally to propose appropriate responses using the economic theories and frameworks.
Critically evaluate the environmental impact of decision-making using the economic approach and policy.
Communicate findings and solutions using diverse formats.

assessments.due_date:
Stage 1 (Week 4): Due online at the end of your sign-on tutorial. 17/08/2026 - 21/08/2026

Stage 2 (Week 10): Due online on Friday at 4pm. 9/10/2026

Stage 3 (Week 11/12/13): Due online at the end of your sign-on showcase presentation. 12/10/2026 - 30/10/2026

Stage 1 and Stage 3 are individual submissions, whereas Stage 2 is a group submission. Only one video recording is required per group.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment task requires students to work collaboratively in groups of four (4) to five (5) to design, produce, and present a podcast on a selected environmental issue. The topic must align with the key lecture themes covered in Weeks 10, 11, and 12.

This task is designed to develop students’ skills in communication, teamwork, critical thinking, and project management, while also providing an opportunity to create a professional-quality podcast as part of their academic portfolio.

The assessment consists of three stages:

Stage 1: Podcast Proposal (10%)

Students will form groups and develop an initial proposal during their allocated tutorial in Week 4. Each student is required to submit an individual proposal using the prescribed template via Turnitin on Blackboard by the end of the sign-on tutorial.

Three main themes will be provided for this assessment. Each theme will have a limited number of groups, and students must enrol in their preferred theme via Blackboard. Further details regarding theme allocation and the sign-up process will be provided in the first lecture.

The proposal stage allows students to generate and explore ideas within their group, which will inform the development of the final group podcast.

Stage 2: Podcast Recording Submission (15%)

Groups are required to produce a 10-minute podcast recording based on their selected topic. The recording must be submitted by Friday of Week 10.

This stage involves consolidating ideas developed during the proposal phase into a cohesive group output.

Buddycheck will be used to support peer evaluation and will be due at the same time as the podcast submission.

Stage 3: Podcast Showcase and Reflection (5%)

Podcast showcase sessions will be conducted during Weeks 11–13, depending on topic allocation. Each group will present their podcast during their assigned session and participate in a Q&A discussion.

Following the presentation, each student must submit an individual reflection via Turnitin on Blackboard during their allocated tutorial.

Further details for each stage will be provided on Blackboard. Students should refer to the task sheet for comprehensive instructions and assessment criteria.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission is via blackboard. Refer to Blackboard for more details.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date: The exam will take place in class on Wednesday, 23 September 2026, at 10:00 am. Further details will be announced on Blackboard.
assessments.task_description: The in-semester exam will assess students’ understanding of analytical tools, environmental analysis, and environmental policy topics covered in Lectures 1 to 8 and Tutorials 1 to 7. The exam will consist of two parts: Part A will include multiple-choice questions, while Part B will comprise short-answer, essay, and/or problem-solving questions. The exam will be conducted in class during the Week 9 lecture period and will have a duration of 60 minutes.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
Students are required to complete an individual Work-Integrated Learning (WIL) applied case study assignment worth 35% of their final grade. The task sheet and dataset will be released in Week 11, and the Industry Expert will introduce and brief the case study during the Week 12 lecture, including a Q&A session.

The assignment comprises a main task (spreadsheet analysis and written report) worth 30% and a reflective component worth 5%.

The assignment must be submitted electronically through the Online Submission link in the Assessment section of the Course Blackboard site. Further details to be announced in Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission is via blackboard. Refer to Blackboard for more details.

Tutorial

Tutorials will be conducted from Week 2 to Week 13. During tutorials, students will complete exercises designed to support exam preparation and participate in podcast-related activities in class. Podcast group discussions and showcase presentations will be held during Weeks 4, 11, 12, and 13.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
